metadata.title: Meat as benign, meat as risk: Mapping news discourse of an ambiguous issue

abstract: This article analyzes the content of news coverage of the meat industry. As knowledge develops about the social problems connected to the meat industry, we are presented with an empirical question. When an everyday consumer item and major commodity with a rich cultural tradition is also linked to potential risks, how do we talk about it? We investigate how risks of the North American meat industry are discussed in newspaper and news blog articles by conducting an automated content analysis of 2461 articles published from May 2015 to August 2016 and qualitatively coding 100 of these articles. We identify which topics predominate in the discussion of meat and also find variation in the extent to which topics are oriented to discussion of social problem risks. Moreover, while newspapers and blogs are each diverse in the way they treat news about the meat industry, we find that social problem risks are relatively de-emphasized in newspapers relative to news blogs. We conclude by discussing the broader implications of our findings and methods for other "taken for granted" phenomena.

sections.paper_id: Bateman et al. - 2019 - Meat as benign, meat as risk Mapping news discourse of an ambiguous issue
paragraphs: Meat is a major commodity in North America and the majority of consumers eat meat regularly. 1 In this sense, meat is "normal." Producing meat is big business, and consuming meat is a tradition with cultural resonance and popularity. In recent decades, though, activists and scientists alike have been increasingly communicating about the problems and risks associated with the industrialization of meat production and meat consumption. Meat has the potential to be both ordinary, and therefore benign, and extraordinary, because it is problematic. To investigate meat's paradoxical status as both a pleasure and problem, we empirically assess how meat is covered in newspapers and news blogs. We pay special attention to how social problems and risks are incorporated in the coverage. In addition to providing a comprehensive analysis of meat news coverage, this analysis is also a case study of the semiotic tensions inherent in the framing of social problems.

In the social construction of social problems and risks, news media play a central role (Beck, 1992;Kasperson et al., 1988). Some issues are relatively tightly framed as risky and/or as a social problem, such as nuclear energy (Gamson & Modigliani, 1989) or climate change (Broadbent et al., 2016). Other issues in the news are framed as important or interesting, although not necessarily as risky or problematic. For example, coverage of politics can be framed as democratically relevant, but not as a social problem or as a risk. The news also includes a large amount of coverage that is meant to satisfy audience demand for 'soft' news, covering topics like sports, lifestyle, and entertainment.

The production and consumption of meat is an exceptionally broad topic, with potential links to culture, economics, politics, employment, health, science, and the environment. Within the news about meat, which topics are most salient? Furthermore, when these topics arise, how salient are discussions of the problems and risks of meat, especially given the pleasures that many people still associate with consuming meat? To answer these questions, we employ topic modeling to map the most prominent topics in news coverage of meat in North America. We also qualitatively code a subsample of articles from these topics to measure the prominence of discussion of risks and problems within them. We further compare coverage in mainstream newspapers with coverage in news blogs as a means to more comprehensively map the dominant discourses on meat and to provide insights into why coverage of risks and problems can vary. In answering these questions about meat, we are able to speak to the broader issue of how news coverage can emphasize or downplay risks, and in so doing affect how problems are socially constructed in the public sphere.

sections.header_raw: The meat industry in the United States and Canada
sections.header_norm: the meat industry in the united states and canada
paragraphs: The risks associated with the meat commodity chain are typically associated with front-line workers, animals in the industrial meat system, human health, and environmental systems (present and future). In academic discourse, risks to workers during the transport, slaughter, and processing of animals in the industrial meat system are commonly discussed (Gouevia & Juska, 2002;Kandel & Parrado, 2005;Pachirat, 2011) alongside the suffering of animals induced by requirements for efficiency in large-scale industrial operations (Pachirat, 2011;Striffler, 2005). Health risks long-associated with consuming meat, such as listeriosis and bacterial contamination (Juska, Gouveia, Gabriel, & Stanley, 2003), have been increasingly supplemented with worry about antibiotic-resistant bacteria (Mathew, Cissell, & Liamthong, 2007) associated with confined animal feeding operations where animals routinely consume antibiotics in their feed. In addition, the consumption of red meat, and especially processed meat, has been associated with increased risk of cancer -a risk that has been highlighted by the World Health Organization (e.g., Bouvard et al., 2015).

Meat production is also discussed as a significant driver of climate change, partly because feed crops like corn require substantial fossil fuels and chemical inputs (Bajželj et al., 2014;IPCC, 2007;Wynes & Nicholas, 2017). Cattle and other ruminants also release a potent greenhouse gas, methane, as a byproduct of digestion (McMichael, Powles, Butler, & Uauy, 2007) and in the course of livestock production, nitrous oxide is released in a larger proportion than in crop production (Davidson, 2012). The production of meat is understood to be powerfully linked to climate change, with conservative estimates suggesting that livestock account for 14.5% of human-induced greenhouse gas emissions (Gerber et al., 2013). These emissions are often discussed as risks (Hannigan, 2014); indeed, climate change risks have already been linked to weather events and patterns that disrupt coastal communities, undermine agricultural productivity, and threaten the integrity of ecosystems (Dunlap & Brulle, 2015).

Despite a wide discussion of these risks, demand for meat continues to increase globally (OECD-FAO, 2016, 2017). The appeal of meat has many dimensions: meat is used to construct masculinity (Adams, 2015;Cairns & Johnston, 2015); demonstrate cosmopolitan taste (Cappeliez & Johnston, 2013); elevate one's status as a "foodie" (Johnston & Baumann, 2007); demonstrate membership in religious (Bonne & Verbeke, 2008) and national (DeSoucey, 2010) groups; and maintain traditional mores and social practices (Chiles, 2017;Waarden, 2006). Meat demand has been psychologically understood through the lens of four rationalizations, or the "four Ns"-meat as necessary, normal, natural, and nice (Piazza et al., 2015). "Necessary" refers to perceived health benefits of meat; "normal" to the social norms related to meat; "natural" suggests that humans have evolved eating meat; and "nice" refers to the pleasurable tastes of meat.

Meat not only plays a central role in everyday consumption practices, but the meat industry also serves as a source of livelihood for many. In the production sector alone, beef, pork, and broiler chicken (chickens raised for meat) accounted for $18 billion in revenue among 41,757 farms in Canada in 2016 (Agriculture & Agri-Food Canada, 2017;Statistics Canada, 2017) and $76.4 billion among 829,795 farms in the United States in 2012 (USDA, 2014). The connection between meat and economic livelihoods complicate any calls for a reduction in meat consumption, given the political salience of issues like employment, rural livelihoods, and economic growth.

To understand the increasing production and consumption of meat (OECD-FAO, 2016, 2017) alongside the intensification of meat risks (Gerber et al., 2013), it is instructive to investigate the framing of meat in public discourse. Given the prominent role of the media in mediating public perceptions of physical risks (Beck [1986] 1992, 2006Beck, 2006Beck [1986] 1992, 2006Beck, 2006Beck [1986] 1992, 2006), analyzing media discourse can highlight common modes of risk distortion but also reveal ways that risks are uncovered and amplified in the public sphere.

sections.paper_id: Bateman et al. - 2019 - Meat as benign, meat as risk Mapping news discourse of an ambiguous issue
sections.header_raw: Media framing, social problems, and risk
sections.header_norm: media framing social problems and risk
paragraphs: The literature on media framing of social problems is premised on the idea that the media are central actors in identifying and defining certain issues as social problems, which are social conditions perceived to be negative for and/or disruptive to society (Best, 2011). In turn, media frames shape audience perceptions and behaviors (Scheufele & Tewksbury, 2007). A related literature on the presentation of risk in the media identifies the media as the key mechanism for filtering, shaping, and conveying information about risks, which are conceptualized as understandings of the probability and magnitude of future harms (Kaplan & John Garrick, 1981). While these literatures are often in dialogue, it is worth analytically distinguishing social problems from risks. Social problems can be perceived as entities that exist apart from an understanding of risk (e.g., sexism), and they can also be perceived as fundamentally entwined with risk discourse (e.g., childhood obesity). The distinction between social problems and risk rests, in part, on a distinction between the ontological position of social problems as existing in the present, as compared with the ways that risks relate to perceptions of future harms. A social problem can be morally repugnant, and it may -but does not necessarily -contain a risk element suggesting the possibility of future harm. For example, both sexual harassment and climate change can be viewed as social problems; while both can be considered morally concerning, climate change is much more closely linked to a discourse of risk suggesting the possibilities of future social and ecological harms.

Perceptions of the presence and nature of social problems is influenced by media framing. Framing involves highlighting salient dimensions of complex issues, and in so doing, defines an issue's causes, consequences, and solutions (Entman, 1993). One premise of this research is that important issues are always multi-dimensional and frames selectively emphasize certain dimensions and downplay others. For example, Savelsberg and Brehm (2015) study Western news coverage of mass violence in the Darfur region and find that the violence is framed as criminal in nature only after interventions by the UN Security Council and the International Criminal Court. Prior research on media framing of social problems tends to take an analytical strategy of selecting news articles related to a predefined issue, and then identifying frames through hand coding by researchers. For example, Riles et al. (2017) study news stories of cancer and find that a "medical" frame predominates, and attributes the causes of cancer to uncontrollable biological or genetic factors; the second most common "lifestyle" frame blames cancer on choices made by individuals, and the third most common "environmental" frame locates causes of cancer in environmental factors beyond an individual's control. Sometimes frames are identified deductively based on prior literature, as in the case above, and sometimes the frames are identified inductively from the sample of news stories being analyzed (e.g., Carlson, 2016).

There is another important reason that public perceptions of risks are intimately connected to media frames: contemporary risks (e.g., changes in atmospheric carbon dioxide) are often imperceptible to the average individual-that is, until they become actualized as catastrophes (e.g., flooding linked to climate change) or personal tragedies (e.g., cancer linked to agricultural pesticides) (Beck, [1986(Beck, [ ] 1992(Beck, [ , 2006:61):61). Knowledge of these imperceptible risks is usually conveyed through discourse by experts, from scientists quoted in media articles to industry lobbyists writing apparently scientific books (Beck, [1986(Beck, [ ] 1992(Beck, [ , 2006:54;:54;Jacques, Dunlap, & Freeman, 2008). For example, news media coverage of the issue of climate change can frame the issue as essentially scientific in nature, and in so doing validates scientific assessments of the likelihood and scale of ecological risks from climate change (Broadbent et al., 2016). The average individual will remain unaware of such risks in the absence of the communication of scientific evidence about these issues as social problems that will cause future harm.

While media framing techniques remain popular and academically relevant, with the advent of computational methods in recent decades, a different strategy for textual analysis has become common. One specialization of computational methods is the identification of patterns in large data sets. Popular tools for pattern finding include automated counts of issue coverage quantity based on pre-defined dictionaries of indicators (e.g., Gorman & Seguin, 2015), measurement of text tone through pre-validated dictionaries (e.g., Bail, Brown, & Mann, 2017), and mapping of word adjacencies which identifies centrality and density of relationships (e.g., Lee & Martin, 2015). Topic modeling, one of the most common pattern-finding techniques, identifies latent topics in a text corpus through an identification of word frequency and co-occurrence, allowing researchers to characterize the predominant substance of a corpus (e.g., Farrell, 2016). These computational methods have the great advantages of being relatively uninfluenced by researcher preconceptions and applying to large sample sizes that could not feasibly be analyzed by human coders. However, as DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei (2013) note, pattern-finding is only a first step in frame identification, as the patterns themselves cannot always sufficiently meet framing's goals of identifying problem definitions, causes, consequences and solutions. While the majority of past research on social problem coverage in the media has been focused specifically on how a clearly identified problem (e.g., poverty) is framed in the media, newer computational research has looked at texts not focused on social problems (e.g., Lee & Martin, 2015), identifying patterns in news coverage of a specific issue or policy domain like arts funding (e.g., DiMaggio et al., 2013).

In this article, we seek to build on media framing studies of social problems and computational method scholarship in order to better understand the ambiguous cultural artifact that is meat. As noted above, meat is a food product that has links to social problems (e.g., cancer) and risks (future climate change), but is also broadly enjoyed, culturally celebrated, and consumed by most of the population. To put it somewhat differently, meat is problematic, but does not seem to be clearly or consistently understood as a social problem. This raises the question of how meat is framed in the media -when is it risky, and when is it simply enjoyable? To address this question, we use computational techniques on a large corpus of newspaper and blog articles, and then proceed to qualitative coding in order to better understand how meat is framed as risky or not. We turn next to a discussion of our corpus and methods.

While media framing techniques remain popular and academically relevant, with the advent of computational methods in recent decades, a different strategy for textual analysis has become common. One specialization of computational methods is the identification of patterns in large data sets. Popular tools for pattern finding include automated counts of issue coverage quantity based on pre-defined dictionaries of indicators (e.g., Gorman & Seguin, 2015), measurement of text tone through pre-validated dictionaries (e.g., Bail, Brown, & Mann, 2017), and mapping of word adjacencies which identifies centrality and density of relationships (e.g., Lee & Martin, 2015). Topic modeling, one of the most common pattern-finding techniques, identifies latent topics in a text corpus through an identification of word frequency and co-occurrence, allowing researchers to characterize the predominant substance of a corpus (e.g., Farrell, 2016). These computational methods have the great advantages of being relatively uninfluenced by researcher preconceptions and applying to large sample sizes that could not feasibly be analyzed by human coders. However, as DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei (2013) note, pattern-finding is only a first step in frame identification, as the patterns themselves cannot always sufficiently meet framing's goals of identifying problem definitions, causes, consequences and solutions. While the majority of past research on social problem coverage in the media has been focused specifically on how a clearly identified problem (e.g., poverty) is framed in the media, newer computational research has looked at texts not focused on social problems (e.g., Lee & Martin, 2015), identifying patterns in news coverage of a specific issue or policy domain like arts funding (e.g., DiMaggio et al., 2013).

In this article, we seek to build on media framing studies of social problems and computational method scholarship in order to better understand the ambiguous cultural artifact that is meat. As noted above, meat is a food product that has links to social problems (e.g., cancer) and risks (future climate change), but is also broadly enjoyed, culturally celebrated, and consumed by most of the population. To put it somewhat differently, meat is problematic, but does not seem to be clearly or consistently understood as a social problem. This raises the question of how meat is framed in the media -when is it risky, and when is it simply enjoyable? To address this question, we use computational techniques on a large corpus of newspaper and blog articles, and then proceed to qualitative coding in order to better understand how meat is framed as risky or not. We turn next to a discussion of our corpus and methods.

The literature on media framing of social problems is premised on the idea that the media are central actors in identifying and defining certain issues as social problems, which are social conditions perceived to be negative for and/or disruptive to society (Best, 2011). In turn, media frames shape audience perceptions and behaviors (Scheufele & Tewksbury, 2007). A related literature on the presentation of risk in the media identifies the media as the key mechanism for filtering, shaping, and conveying information about risks, which are conceptualized as understandings of the probability and magnitude of future harms (Kaplan & John Garrick, 1981). While these literatures are often in dialogue, it is worth analytically distinguishing social problems from risks. Social problems can be perceived as entities that exist apart from an understanding of risk (e.g., sexism), and they can also be perceived as fundamentally entwined with risk discourse (e.g., childhood obesity). The distinction between social problems and risk rests, in part, on a distinction between the ontological position of social problems as existing in the present, as compared with the ways that risks relate to perceptions of future harms. A social problem can be morally repugnant, and it may -but does not necessarily -contain a risk element suggesting the possibility of future harm. For example, both sexual harassment and climate change can be viewed as social problems; while both can be considered morally concerning, climate change is much more closely linked to a discourse of risk suggesting the possibilities of future social and ecological harms.

Perceptions of the presence and nature of social problems is influenced by media framing. Framing involves highlighting salient dimensions of complex issues, and in so doing, defines an issue's causes, consequences, and solutions (Entman, 1993). One premise of this research is that important issues are always multi-dimensional and frames selectively emphasize certain dimensions and downplay others. For example, Savelsberg and Brehm (2015) study Western news coverage of mass violence in the Darfur region and find that the violence is framed as criminal in nature only after interventions by the UN Security Council and the International Criminal Court. Prior research on media framing of social problems tends to take an analytical strategy of selecting news articles related to a predefined issue, and then identifying frames through hand coding by researchers. For example, Riles et al. (2017) study news stories of cancer and find that a "medical" frame predominates, and attributes the causes of cancer to uncontrollable biological or genetic factors; the second most common "lifestyle" frame blames cancer on choices made by individuals, and the third most common "environmental" frame locates causes of cancer in environmental factors beyond an individual's control. Sometimes frames are identified deductively based on prior literature, as in the case above, and sometimes the frames are identified inductively from the sample of news stories being analyzed (e.g., Carlson, 2016).

There is another important reason that public perceptions of risks are intimately connected to media frames: contemporary risks (e.g., changes in atmospheric carbon dioxide) are often imperceptible to the average individual-that is, until they become actualized as catastrophes (e.g., flooding linked to climate change) or personal tragedies (e.g., cancer linked to agricultural pesticides) (Beck, [1986(Beck, [ ] 1992(Beck, [ , 2006:61):61). sections.paper_id: Bateman et al. - 2019 - Meat as benign, meat as risk Mapping news discourse of an ambiguous issue
Bateman et al. - 2019 - Meat as benign, meat as risk Mapping news discourse of an ambiguous issue
paragraphs: We began by identifying a broad corpus of articles that included both mainstream newspapers and blogs. Including both kinds of sources enabled us to capture a greater breadth of public discourse, especially when the number of media voices is proliferating beyond traditional news outlets. Furthermore, blogs are often institutionally leaner than newspapers, which are large, complex organizations with high costs (George, 2007;Schudson, 1989). While analysis of news in mainstream newspapers is an established research tradition, there is relatively little research on news in blogs. It is important to emphasize the highly varied terrain of the contemporary blogosphere, which defies many people's prototypical expectations of individually-authored, personal blogs. Although there are still individual-focused blogs (e.g., food blogs), there are also a wide range of other blog formats, such as blogs that are part of "legacy media" like newspapers experimenting with relatively open blog forms; blogs that resemble digital versions of newspapers (e.g., The Huffington Post); blogs that are part of other large organizations (e.g., the USDA); and "digitally native" blogs written by organizations with only an online presence (e.g., The Alternative Daily). The lower operating costs of blogs usually means that they are not behind paywalls, making them easy to link with Twitter, Facebook, or other social media posts (e.g., Poell, 2014). Relatedly, social media have overtaken newspapers as the primary platform for news consumption and will likely soon overtake television (Pew Research Center, 2017). 2 With an increasing amount of news from social media and declining newspaper readership (e.g., Pew Research Center, 2016), assessing the discussion of meat risks on blogs can help us understand the topics readers encounter when accessing various forms of news media.

We selected newspapers through consideration of readership size (a range from ∼180,000 to 1000,000 daily circulation) and distribution in different geographic areas of the United States and Canada (see Table 1). These included national newspapers (The Globe & Mail, The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal, The Washington Post, USA Today) and regional newspapers with wide circulation (Los Angeles Times, Minneapolis Star-Tribune, Toronto Star). Articles were selected from newspapers by searching with our search term (see below) each newspaper individually in ProQuest's US News Stream and Canadian News Stream databases. ProQuest aggregates news publications and is available through an institutional subscription, and results provide full text of all articles returned by the search term. 3 We collected articles spanning 16 months (May 2015 to August 2016). 4 The newspaper corpus contains 1289 articles.

Blogs were sampled by entering the search term into Google's blog database, with settings adjusted to avoid tailored search results (details in the appendix). In cases where the blog was not headquartered in the US or Canada, the article had to address a general aspect of meat to potentially qualify for relevance (e.g., one article in the blog corpus was written by Greenpeace Africa but discussed the general environmental impacts of large-scale agriculture). Google's blog database was used because it is (as of the time of writing) the only database of general blogs calibrated by many users, so the search results provide articles that are not only relevant but also read. 5 The database indexes not only blogs written by individual people, but also blogs written by organizations. Notably, almost all of the articles in our blog corpus were written by organizations, such as the USDA, the blog section of newspapers (e.g., the New York Times blogs), or online-only organizations (e.g., Elite Daily, Food Tank). There were 308 unique blogs in our sample; the ten blogs most prominently represented in our sample are listed in Table 2. The total size of the blog sample is 1172.

The samples were constructed through the first step of identifying articles through a search term, which was designed to collect newspaper and blog articles spanning the meat commodity chain, from production to processing, distribution, retailing, and consumption. 6 It was generic enough to gather articles about all topics of meat and different sectors of the meat industry. The search term also included keywords intended to find articles about meat alternatives, but these articles were only included in our corpora if the meat alternatives were discussed in relation to chicken, beef, or pork. We constrained the sample by limiting "meat" to chicken, beef, and pork, because these are the most commonly produced and consumed forms of meat in the United States and Canada (Agriculture & Agri-Food Canada, 2017;USDA, 2014). Limiting the samples to articles about these forms of meat also allowed the topic of the study to remain manageable thematically-discussing the various elements of the chicken, beef, and pork industries allowed for greater analytical depth than discussing the much wider array of issues related to meat (e.g., those associated with fois gras, game meat, etc.). Since Google searches allow for only 1000 search results per search, we used several general search terms in separate searches of the blog database (this is why the primary operator in the search term is "OR"). A drawback of the search term was that it returned many irrelevant articles. We assessed whether each blog and newspaper article collected by the search term (just over 10,000 articles in the blog database and 3166 in the newspaper database) referred to meat generally or chicken, beef, or pork. To do so, we read each newspaper and blog article individually to assess whether it was relevant to the meat industry. 7 Following deletion 2 While social media are the platform through which audiences are encountering news, the sources of the news audiences find on social media are diverse and include online versions of newspaper articles.

3 ProQuest returns batches of full text results of 100 articles. Each article was manually and individually converted to plain text and saved as a separate plain text file, which is a requirement for topic modelling in R. 4 The time frame was constrained by the technical limitations of Google's blog search function, which allows for a maximum search of posts one year back from the time of the search. Because the data collection effort spanned a time period of four months, we were able to collect 16 months of posts in total. We set the newspaper search for the same time period so that we could compare the sources. Our newspaper selection was also affected by the availability of full text articles. Some publications we would have liked to include were not available through our institutional subscription. However, we argue that our current sample is broad and diverse, and we would not expect to see our results differ substantially from the inclusion of other major newspapers. 5 Google is a "web crawler". It is possible to create a custom web crawler, but the algorithms that Google uses to suggest relevant web pages in search results are sensitive to the pages that other users have accessed. This makes Google search results a better measure of what online users are reading than a custom web crawler. We also explored using the blog search function in Factiva, but Factiva only indexes a particular selection of blogs directed toward a business audience and the results are not tuned by users. 6 The search term aimed to reach articles about the meat industry in general and the specific sectors of the meat industry: meat industry OR "beef industry" OR "pork industry" OR "chicken industry" OR "poultry industry" OR "pig industry" OR "cow industry" OR "meat production" OR "beef production" OR "cow production" OR "pig production" OR "pork production" OR "chicken production" OR "poultry production" OR "meat consumption" OR "beef consumption" OR "pork consumption" OR "chicken consumption" OR "poultry consumption" OR meat production OR beef production OR pork production OR chicken production OR poultry production OR meat consumption OR beef consumption OR pork consumption OR chicken consumption OR poultry consumption OR "meat alternative" OR "meat alternatives" OR "meat retailing" OR "meat retail" OR meat retail OR beef retail OR pork retail OR chicken retail OR poultry retail OR "meat distribution" OR meat distribution OR beef distribution OR pork distribution OR chicken distribution OR poultry distribution 7 Examples of articles that were captured by the search term but not relevant to the meat industry include articles that use the common phrase "a chicken and egg problem" or that refer to the musician Meatloaf.
of articles unrelated to the meat industry, our newspaper corpus contained 1289 articles and our blog corpus contained 1172 articles. 8

paragraphs: To analyze how meat in Canada and the United States is discussed, we began by using topic modeling to identify the prevalence of topics related to meat in blogs and in mainstream newspapers separately. Topic modeling is an unsupervised automated content analysis method (e.g., Mohr & Bogdanov, 2013) that derives the latent structures -topics -underlying a set of texts (Blei, 2012;Blei, Ng, & Jordan, 2003;Marshall, 2013). Each document is treated as a "bag of words", meaning that during pre-processing, the document set is reduced to a matrix with one document in each row and one word in each column, with the body of the matrix composed of integers indicating how many times each word appears in each document. With the document as a bags of words, the documents are considered "effects", with topics as the "causes" of these documents. Topic models require large sets of documents ("corpora"), because co-occurrences of words are determined on a per-document basis. Each topic is returned as a list of words that consistently co-occur in documents in the corpus. The same word can appear in several topics, a provision that allows topic models to incorporate polysemy (e.g., "organic" can take on its meaning of a method of production in one topic and simply "the natural way to do something" in another). The topics often make intuitive sense and aid in deriving a coarse meaning structure for a particular corpus (DiMaggio et al., 2013;Jockers & Mimno, 2013;Marshall, 2013;Mohr, Wagner-Pacifici, Breiger, & Bogdanov, 2013).

We used R (CRAN, 2017) and the lda function of the topic models package (Grün & Hornik, 2011) to run topic models (using Gibbs sampling). During pre-processing, we removed numbers, made all text lowercase, and removed stopwords, punctuation, and additional whitespace. The stopword dictionary was constructed by supplementing the stock stopwords dictionary with the most common words in each corpus (returned as lists) and removing words with no potential for substantive interpretation (e.g., "rather"). Then topic models were run and new words were removed. 9 Topic modeling reduces the degree of behind-the-scenes interpretive work often characteristic of qualitative coding (Biernacki, 2012;Lee & Martin, 2015;Martin, 2017). While the process of deriving topics is automated with topic modeling, several steps requiring a measure of subjective judgement nevertheless arise, including the sample parameters and search term, the decision of the number of topics, and the inductively derived topic labels for each string of words comprising a topic. To maintain topic modeling's The Appendix includes a complete list of blogs used in the sample. 8 The discrepancy in the proportion of relevant blog articles vs. newspaper articles is due to the fact that the search term was applied in the Google search not only to the text of blog articles but also to links included on the webpage being searched. Often, the titles of relevant articles were included as links on the sidebar of otherwise irrelevant articles. 9 The list of stopwords is in the Appendix.

promise of providing sociology with a method for increasing the reliability of coded themes (DiMaggio et al., 2013;Mohr et al., 2013), these steps must be made explicit. See the Appendix for details on our model selection decisions and for tests of robustness.

After computationally deriving a range of topic numbers (2016b, Arun, Suresh, Madhavan, & Naramisha Murty, 2010;Devaud, Juan, & Bellot, 2014;Griffiths & Steyvers, 2004;Juan, Tian, Jintao, & Yongdong, 2008;Murzintcev, 2016a, Murzintcev, 2016c, 2016b, 2016c), we concluded that ten topics for each corpus optimized interpretability and allowed an exploration of each topic in sufficient depth. These ten topics indicated the broad frames used to understand the complexity of meat.

sections.paper_id: Bateman et al. - 2019 - Meat as benign, meat as risk Mapping news discourse of an ambiguous issue
paragraphs: As other researchers have noted, there are advantages to "hybrid" methods that combine automated textual analysis with techniques that employ human judgement (Su et al., 2016). In our case, topic modelling provided a description of the most salient topics, which we then validated and refined through qualitative analysis.

In order to validate the topic names, the authors read and collectively discussed the five articles the LDA model identified as most representative of each topic. As there were 10 topics each for the newspaper and blog corpora, we read 100 articles in depth. 10 The qualitative coding allowed us to name the topics more conclusively-typically, the lists of words returned by topic modeling are the primary data used to assign topic names, but qualitative readings allowed us to create more valid names (in several instances, a topic name was assigned that did not appear in the topic list, but nonetheless represented the general themes presented).

We used the topics provided by the topic modelling to guide the second step of qualitative coding of this subsample of 100 articles. The topic modeling produced topics that we interpreted in relation to the literature on social problems in the news, which lead us to read the articles as predominantly focused on meat either as a benign consumer good or as a potential source of risk. Therefore, in this second qualitative step, we focused our reading of the articles on the framing of meat as a social good, a risk, or both. To code each article by type of risk, the authors first read the articles individually and then collectively discussed how or whether risk was discussed. This process produced a single risk code for each article. We used this qualitative reading to categorize articles connected to specific topics in each corpora, in order to compare how blogs and newspapers discussed risks to varying degrees. As a final step, we used word counts of key words that are ostensibly indicative of risk in order to validate our qualitative reading.

sections.paper_id: Bateman et al. - 2019 - Meat as benign, meat as risk Mapping news discourse of an ambiguous issue
sections.header_raw: Findings: the broad contours of meat discourse
sections.header_norm: findings the broad contours of meat discourse
paragraphs: Table 3 presents the topic lists created by the latent Dirichlet analysis for the ten-topic solution for the newspaper corpus. The table includes the 15 words most strongly associated with each topic. Table 4 presents the analogous information for the blog corpus. 11 Topics are arranged according to the proportion of the corpus that belongs to each topic.

Looking first at the topics in the newspaper articles, we identified ten key topics (which we capitalize) in the discourse: five are related to, or based on, meat production (Profit Seeking in Big Agriculture, Trade and Government, Producing Poultry, Food and Agriculture, Meat Makers), while another four center on meat consumers (Health, Daily Life, Food Consumers, The Neighborhood Scene). 12 The tenth topic, Gastronomy, spans both consumer and producer issues. The production topics are typically more concerned with the financial or regulatory dimensions of producing and consuming meat, while the consumer topics are typically concerned with more cultural dimensions. The topic of Health stands out as a consumption topic which has less obvious thematic overlap with other topics; rather than relating to culture or to pleasure, this topic highlights individual concerns and behaviors connected to meat. Overall, these topics provide an intriguing picture of the main concerns of the newspaper discourse on meat. Meat's various roles in society are present in the discourse: as a valuable commodity for which there is an international market, as a cultural item implicated in social practices and relationships, and as a food that has connections to human health. Within both the production and consumerfocused topics, we see how meat can be discussed as a quotidian topic, one that is not problematized. At the same time, other topics, like Health, more readily raise the prospect of various risks.

We found interesting overlaps with and contrasts between the newspaper topics and the blog topics. We identify ten key topics in the blog corpus: four topics are related to meat production (Production, Big Agriculture Working Conditions, Environment and Climate Change, Trade and Law), four are related to consumer issues (Health Risks, Healthy Diet, Finding Delicious Food, Avoiding Meat), and two topics transcend consumer and producer concerns (Food Safety, Natural and Quality). We see in these topics similar spans across production and consumption, and coverage that identifies meat's cultural, commodity, and regulatory dimensions. Health was discussed across both newspapers and blogs, associated with terms such as "cancer," "processed meat," "research," and 10 We arrived at the decision to read five articles in depth per topic through balancing the need to keep the data collection feasible with the need to understand what the topics were and the risk patterns contained in them. We found that in most cases five articles allowed us to confidently identify the topics and the social problem and business risks they contained. In cases where we were not confident from a reading of five articles, we also consulted the next several articles that were most highly associated with those topics. 11 We first ran the topic models on the newspaper and blog corpora separately to find our ten topics specific to each corpora. As a further validation of our method, we also ran a structural topic model. The details of that method and the results are included in the Appendix. The results corroborate our initial findings. 12 Readers will notice that "Trump" appears under the "Food and Agriculture" topic and not the "Trade and Government Topic". Since our data window runs from May 2015 to August 2016, we only have data from Trump during the campaign, and not as a governmental representative or source of policy. At this point, Trump was raising populist issues, but not actually governing.

"evidence." The production and market aspects of the industry are also discussed across both newspaper and blog sources with common terms such as "industry", "market", "sales," "price," and "costs". Consumer tastes and preferences are also common across newspaper and blog sources, with many articles related to consumer tastes and practices frequently using "organic", "grocery," "home," "life," "wine," "cooking" and other related terms emphasizing the place of meat in the course of daily life. Topics appeared in both sources related to restaurants and eating out. Other topics appeared in both sources for trade and legislative issues, and also for the political aspects of large agricultural corporations. Notably, topics for the environment and animal welfare (Avoiding Meat) only appeared in the blogs. Voices from sources such as Equation (the Union of Concerned Scientists blog) and People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals (PETA) did not appear as frequently in the newspaper articles as in the blog articles.

The topics provided by latent Dirichlet analysis are extremely valuable for describing in broad terms how meat is discussed in the news and describing similarities and differences between newspapers and blogs. Specifically, we learn that sometimes meat topics seem distant from discussions of risks and problems, when issues of cultural production, enjoyment, profitability, and social connections come to the fore. At other points in the discourse, especially within blogs, the social problems connected to meat seem to be more central -its potential impact on the environment, on human health, and on workers. The method cannot, however, provide a clear picture of how risks are framed -within each topic, we do not know how risks are emphasized or downplayed, or what kinds of risks are discussed. In other words, are the potentially problematic topics framed as problems?

To produce a clearer picture about risk, we turn to our qualitative analysis of the top five most representative articles (defined by the LDA model) for the ten blog topics and ten newspapers topics (n = 100 articles). We inductively identified three categories of the Note: The topic proportions indicate the proportion of the corpus that belongs to each topic.

discussion of risk: 1) articles with a discussion of risks affecting profits in the meat industry; and 2) articles discussing environmental and social risks resulting from the meat commodity chain; and 3) articles that do not discuss risk. We classified "business risks" (category 1) as those that impact the optimal functioning of the meat industry. A drought affecting cattle producers, or market fluctuations lowering quarterly earnings statements of large meat corporations were classified as business risks. A clear example can be found in a newspaper article which discusses the financial hardships faced by poultry producers as a result of losses to their flocks caused by bird flu. The second category, "social problem risks," involved risks resulting from the meat commodity chain that made harm to individuals, groups, or the environment possible-such as meat's impact on climate change and on health. A good example of a social problem risk is a blog post which details complaints by rural residents whose air and water were being polluted through illegal dumping by a poultry processing plant. Finally, articles with no discussion of business or social problem risks (category 3) were identified as "no-risk". We placed articles in a category based on our readings of each article as a whole (i.e., not line coding). In our qualitative sample, none of the articles discussed business and social problem risks simultaneously. Tables 5 and 6 present the results of that categorization for newspapers and for blogs respectively. These tables reveal the relative prominence of social problems in the blogs versus newspapers, with the latter dominated by discussions of business risks and a greater frequency of "no risk" articles.

To clearly present the comparison of how risks are presented in the newspapers vs. blogs, we constructed Table 7. We assigned each topic to the risk categorization that predominated (i.e, 3 out of 5 articles or greater). Regarding newspapers compared to blogs, blogs included more topics permeated by social problem risks than newspapers. Newspapers present meat largely as a benign (no discussion of risk) or business issue, with articles about health risks being the exception. Blog articles, however, link meat to a more diverse set of social problem risks-animal welfare, the environment and climate change, abuses of workers by large agricultural corporations, and a wider array of health risks.

Our qualitative reading necessarily prioritizes validity over reliability in its coding of risks. Notably, we qualitatively coded only 50 of the 1289 newspaper articles in our sample and only 50 of the 1172 blog articles in our sample. Are the differences we found between them reflective of overall differences between the two samples? As a check on the patterns we identify qualitatively, we conducted words counts within each of our two corpora. We counted words that are associated with the social problem risks and the business risk we found in our coding. The results are presented in Table 8. For all the risk-related words, their prevalence is highersometimes much higher -in the blog sample, with the exception of the word "business," which is more prevalent in the newspaper sample. These word counts support the results of our qualitative coding.

Table 3 presents the topic lists created by the latent Dirichlet analysis for the ten-topic solution for the newspaper corpus. The table includes the 15 words most strongly associated with each topic. Table 4 presents the analogous information for the blog corpus. 11 Topics are arranged according to the proportion of the corpus that belongs to each topic.

Looking first at the topics in the newspaper articles, we identified ten key topics (which we capitalize) in the discourse: five are related to, or based on, meat production (Profit Seeking in Big Agriculture, Trade and Government, Producing Poultry, Food and Agriculture, Meat Makers), while another four center on meat consumers (Health, Daily Life, Food Consumers, The Neighborhood Scene). 12 The tenth topic, Gastronomy, spans both consumer and producer issues. The production topics are typically more concerned with the financial or regulatory dimensions of producing and consuming meat, while the consumer topics are typically concerned with more cultural dimensions. The topic of Health stands out as a consumption topic which has less obvious thematic overlap with other topics; rather than relating to culture or to pleasure, this topic highlights individual concerns and behaviors connected to meat. Overall, these topics provide an intriguing picture of the main concerns of the newspaper discourse on meat. Meat's various roles in society are present in the discourse: as a valuable commodity for which there is an international market, as a cultural item implicated in social practices and relationships, and as a food that has connections to human health. Within both the production and consumerfocused topics, we see how meat can be discussed as a quotidian topic, one that is not problematized. At the same time, other topics, like Health, more readily raise the prospect of various risks.

We found interesting overlaps with and contrasts between the newspaper topics and the blog topics. We identify ten key topics in the blog corpus: four topics are related to meat production (Production, Big Agriculture Working Conditions, Environment and Climate Change, Trade and Law), four are related to consumer issues (Health Risks, Healthy Diet, Finding Delicious Food, Avoiding Meat), and two topics transcend consumer and producer concerns (Food Safety, Natural and Quality). We see in these topics similar spans across production and consumption, and coverage that identifies meat's cultural, commodity, and regulatory dimensions. We found that in most cases five articles allowed us to confidently identify the topics and the social problem and business risks they contained. In cases where we were not confident from a reading of five articles, we also consulted the next several articles that were most highly associated with those topics. 11 We first ran the topic models on the newspaper and blog corpora separately to find our ten topics specific to each corpora. As a further validation of our method, we also ran a structural topic model. The details of that method and the results are included in the Appendix. The results corroborate our initial findings. 12 Readers will notice that "Trump" appears under the "Food and Agriculture" topic and not the "Trade and Government Topic". Since our data window runs from May 2015 to August 2016, we only have data from Trump during the campaign, and not as a governmental representative or source of policy. At this point, Trump was raising populist issues, but not actually governing.

"evidence." The production and market aspects of the industry are also discussed across both newspaper and blog sources with common terms such as "industry", "market", "sales," "price," and "costs". Consumer tastes and preferences are also common across newspaper and blog sources, with many articles related to consumer tastes and practices frequently using "organic", "grocery," "home," "life," "wine," "cooking" and other related terms emphasizing the place of meat in the course of daily life. Topics appeared in both sources related to restaurants and eating out. Other topics appeared in both sources for trade and legislative issues, and also for the political aspects of large agricultural corporations. Voices from sources such as Equation (the Union of Concerned Scientists blog) and People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals (PETA) did not appear as frequently in the newspaper articles as in the blog articles.

discussion of risk: 1) articles with a discussion of risks affecting profits in the meat industry; and 2) articles discussing environmental and social risks resulting from the meat commodity chain; and 3) articles that do not discuss risk. sections.paper_id: Bateman et al. - 2019 - Meat as benign, meat as risk Mapping news discourse of an ambiguous issue
paragraphs: Our research findings aimed to investigate public discourse around meat. What do we talk about when we talk about meat? How is meat's paradoxical status as a pleasurable food item, an important economic commodity, and a potential social problem addressed Note: Bold font indicates predominance of social problem risks.

in public discourse? Through topic modeling, we identified key topics that predominate the discourse in newspapers and blogs. These topics focus on economic business issues related to production, consumer issues relating to taste, culture, and health, and some blogexclusive emphasis on environmental issues and animal welfare. Through qualitative coding, we confirm that newspaper articles, in particular, include very little discussion of the social problem risks of meat, and instead mostly cover meat as a benign issue or as an issue with business risks. We also find that blog articles include more substantial coverage of the social problem risks of meat compared to newspapers, although there is still coverage of the benign dimensions of meat as well as the business risks of meat. In other words, in both sources, meat is both benign and risky. Studies of the news framing of issues such as crime, discrimination, poverty, and pollution can generally assume that these discussions relate to an issue's status as a social problem. In this paper, we take an issue -the meat industry -where we cannot assume a priori a level of problematization. The production and consumption of meat has, for many people, a taken-for-granted status, and it is linked to matters of economics, policy, and culture. At the same time, there have been warnings issued about the risks of meat production and consumption, risks to health, to workers' well-being, to the environment, and to animals. For a liminal topic like meat, our findings demonstrate that most of what newspaper readers see frames meat in ways that obscures the social problems associated with meat. When newspapers did discuss risks, the individualized issue of health was more often discussed than risks that may be framed in terms of collective action such as environmental or labor risks. Discussions of risks that may be framed as issues for collective action may themselves be a "business risk" for newspapers. Even though blogs present meat as a benign object as well as a straightforward business commodity, they also offer a platform where the risks of meat tend to be more freely discussed.

Like other cultural objects that integrate multiple benign and risky elements (Davidson & Gismondi, 2011;Norgaard, 2011; Saguy & Gruys, 2010;Streib, Ayala, & Wixted, 2017;Wahl-Jorgensen, Bennett, & Taylor, 2017), meat can be framed in ways that vary across people and institutions. The polysemy of risky entities, like meat, prevents the recognition of clear lines of action from media about such entities. Since meat is defined in many different ways, and is only sometimes classified as a risk, readers are left without a clear imperative to change their consumption practices or otherwise engage democratically to alter how meat is collectively produced and consumed. Most of the topics we find do not lend themselves to the presentation of a social problem risk.

When meat is routinely presented as non-problematic as an aspect of gastronomy (e.g., a delicious steak), or part of daily life and dynamic neighbourhoods (e.g., a menu feature of a well-regarded restaurant), an overly positive, or at least ambiguous impression may be created in public discourse. Although meat may have problematic qualities that are identified in some sources, public discourse continues to strongly emphasize the many advantages of meat production and consumption, and this likely works to neutralize or eclipse the risky elements in public consciousness.

While there is an overlap in their topics, blogs also focus on different topics than newspapers and have a greater focus on meat's risks. One way to think about the importance of this difference is that the institutional constraints of newspapers often work against covering such issues (Hannigan, 2014;Wahl-Jorgensen et al., 2017), but blogs remain less tied to the news routines and to the (ironically) risk-managing economic models typical of traditional newspapers. Thus the organizational structure of newspapers-large, complex organizations with high costs-partly dictates which stories can be published (George, 2007;Molotch & Lester, 1974;Schudson, 1989;Shoemaker & Reese, 1996;Tuchman, 1973). Concern about advertising revenue, among other factors, leads mainstream media organizations to frequently present risky objects and phenomena as benign (e.g., Davidson & Gismondi, 2011;Norgaard, 2011;Saguy & Gruys, 2010;Streib et al., 2017;Wahl-Jorgensen et al., 2017). While certain blogs are similarly outgrowths of institutionally complex organizations, as an online element of newspapers (e.g., the Wall Street Journal Blog) or other large organizations (e.g., the USDA Blog), others are "digitally native" blogs written by organizations with only an online presence (e.g., Barfblog, a blog about foodborne pathogens) or by individuals (such as the vegan cooking blog Oh She Glows). 13 Institutionally leaner blogs provide a less constrained format for reporting about risky and other phenomena because of institutional flexibility of the format (e.g., flexible digital article formats and flexibility in selecting contributors) and lower costs of publication. These institutional differences often allow blogs and other forms of social media (Manikonda, Beigi, Liu, & Kambhampati, 2018) to provide a platform for a more critical and open discussion of risks (Hookway, 2008;Maratea, 2008;Wahl-Jorgensen et al., 2017). Despite this greater openness, social media can also provide a tool for downplaying risks (Ruggeri & Samoggia, 2018) and can diffuse social movement energy when corporate platforms (such as Twitter) are used (Poell, 2014).

While there are institutional differences between blogs and newspapers, they both aim to respond to major issues that arise. During our study window, the World Health Organization released a report on the link between meat consumption and the risk of cancer (Bouvard et al., 2015). This report received a great deal of media attention in both newspapers and blogs. It is possible that the individualized nature of this social problem risk made it more likely to be taken up by newspapers in contrast to issues such as climate change that require a more collective response. The other major issue that arose during our study window was an outbreak of bird flu. This outbreak was a serious economic challenge for poultry producers, and we saw concerns about this business risk taken up in both blogs and newspapers.

To the extent that the emphasis in the newspaper articles on the benign aspects of meat and business risks reflects a general pattern in newspaper coverage, where newspapers downplay social problem risks relative to blogs, newspapers may not be a leading source for galvanizing action on cultural objects permeated with risk. In contrast, if the news coverage we find in blogs reflects a broader tendency to balance discussion of social problem risks with other coverage, blogs may represent a good source of news frames that raise awareness of and promote action on social problems.

Discussions of cultural phenomena can be understood as permeated with conflicting frames, which, in the context of risky phenomena, may prevent ideas (about e.g., sustainability) from translating into action. Our topics represent the many heterogeneous tools we have to understand meat, which often leave us without clear conclusions about changes in action-whether this is to only buy organic meat, eat less meat, become vegetarian (or otherwise change our meat consumption practices) or seek to change policies and advocate for laws (e.g., the dismantling of "ag gag" laws) through other democratic means. Beyond the institutional constraints of mainstream news, the online world seems to provide greater ability to uncover and amplify the risks associated with risky objects like meat.

Future research could investigate beyond the North American context and beyond the time frame under study here. Other directions for future research could include comparisons of types of newspapers or within types of blogs, and connections between them. More fundamentally, the relationship between the qualitative coding and the topic modeling we propose could be augmented and enhanced through a more extensive interpretive analysis that looks to explore the various meanings (i.e., beyond risks) of the topics identified by the models.

Our methods and specifically our attention to the social construction of risk may be usefully adopted in order to illuminate the media's role in reflecting and reinforcing dominant understandings of how everyday consumer items are benign in the face of information about significant potential harms. For example, the case of sugar is one where information about the potential harms to individuals and to entire populations has recently become more visible. Like meat, sugar has a significant consumption pattern that is near-universal, routine, and linked to everyday pleasures as well as health risks. It is not clear how sugar is presented in 13 While it is beyond the scope of this paper to conduct a full analysis, we note that there are likely important systematic differences in the institutional ties and funding for blog content. Future research should investigate whether these differences are related to the kinds of discourses found in those blogs. public discourse, in terms of a comparison of its risky and benign qualities. Topic modelling and qualitative analysis of the types of risks that appear in media coverage of sugar (e.g., in cooking media) would address this question. The public discourse around alcohol might be more focused on risk, given the attention to the social problems of drunk driving and addiction. The approach employed here could be used to establish how alcohol's risks compare to its presentation as a fun and pleasurable beverage. The plastics industry and plastic consumer items are yet another topic that could be investigated with the approach used here. Plastic has a taken for granted status insofar as it is ubiquitous as a consumer item and is clearly helpful and useful to consumers in myriad ways. At the same time, information about the potentially catastrophic risks posed by plastics to entire species and ecosystems is coming forward. Knowing more about the public discourse of plastic could help us to understand consumer behaviors regarding plastic. In all these cases, we see a divide between the benign goods consumers enjoy (e.g., steaks, sugary desserts, cocktails, and disposable water bottles) and the potential harms that are either impossible to see from an individual's vantage point or are masked, intentionally or unintentionally, from the consumer.

It is possible that the individualized nature of this social problem risk made it more likely to be taken up by newspapers in contrast to issues such as climate change that require a more collective response. The other major issue that arose during our study window was an outbreak of bird flu. This outbreak was a serious economic challenge for poultry producers, and we saw concerns about this business risk taken up in both blogs and newspapers.

To the extent that the emphasis in the newspaper articles on the benign aspects of meat and business risks reflects a general pattern in newspaper coverage, where newspapers downplay social problem risks relative to blogs, newspapers may not be a leading source for galvanizing action on cultural objects permeated with risk. In contrast, if the news coverage we find in blogs reflects a broader tendency to balance discussion of social problem risks with other coverage, blogs may represent a good source of news frames that raise awareness of and promote action on social problems.

Discussions of cultural phenomena can be understood as permeated with conflicting frames, which, in the context of risky phenomena, may prevent ideas (about e.g., sustainability) from translating into action. Our topics represent the many heterogeneous tools we have to understand meat, which often leave us without clear conclusions about changes in action-whether this is to only buy organic meat, eat less meat, become vegetarian (or otherwise change our meat consumption practices) or seek to change policies and advocate for laws (e.g., the dismantling of "ag gag" laws) through other democratic means. Beyond the institutional constraints of mainstream news, the online world seems to provide greater ability to uncover and amplify the risks associated with risky objects like meat.

Future research could investigate beyond the North American context and beyond the time frame under study here. Other directions for future research could include comparisons of types of newspapers or within types of blogs, and connections between them. More fundamentally, the relationship between the qualitative coding and the topic modeling we propose could be augmented and enhanced through a more extensive interpretive analysis that looks to explore the various meanings (i.e., beyond risks) of the topics identified by the models.

Our methods and specifically our attention to the social construction of risk may be usefully adopted in order to illuminate the media's role in reflecting and reinforcing dominant understandings of how everyday consumer items are benign in the face of information about significant potential harms. For example, the case of sugar is one where information about the potential harms to individuals and to entire populations has recently become more visible. Like meat, sugar has a significant consumption pattern that is near-universal, routine, and linked to everyday pleasures as well as health risks. It is not clear how sugar is presented in 13 While it is beyond the scope of this paper to conduct a full analysis, we note that there are likely important systematic differences in the institutional ties and funding for blog content. Future research should investigate whether these differences are related to the kinds of discourses found in those blogs. public discourse, in terms of a comparison of its risky and benign qualities. Topic modelling and qualitative analysis of the types of risks that appear in media coverage of sugar (e.g., in cooking media) would address this question. The public discourse around alcohol might be more focused on risk, given the attention to the social problems of drunk driving and addiction. The approach employed here could be used to establish how alcohol's risks compare to its presentation as a fun and pleasurable beverage. The plastics industry and plastic consumer items are yet another topic that could be investigated with the approach used here. Plastic has a taken for granted status insofar as it is ubiquitous as a consumer item and is clearly helpful and useful to consumers in myriad ways. At the same time, information about the potentially catastrophic risks posed by plastics to entire species and ecosystems is coming forward. Knowing more about the public discourse of plastic could help us to understand consumer behaviors regarding plastic. In all these cases, we see a divide between the benign goods consumers enjoy (e.g., steaks, sugary desserts, cocktails, and disposable water bottles) and the potential harms that are either impossible to see from an individual's vantage point or are masked, intentionally or unintentionally, from the consumer.
